etymology: Greek: neos (new) + didymos (twin) — 'new twin,' because it was the second of the two elements separated from didymium. Its companion praseodymium was the 'green twin'; neodymium was the 'new twin,' separated simultaneously from the same false element.

timeline.event: Mosander discovers 'didymium' within lanthanum oxide and names it for the Greek word for twin, because it is always accompanied by lanthanum. For over four decades, didymium is accepted as a genuine element in the periodic tables of the world.

Carl Auer von Welsbach, working through hundreds of fractional crystallizations of didymium ammonium nitrate, separates two distinct fractions. The pink to violet fraction — neodymium — has spectral lines and colored salts distinct from the green praseodymium fraction. Both are announced simultaneously as new elements.

Pure neodymium metal is produced for the first time in a meaningful quantity by reducing neodymium fluoride with calcium — allowing systematic measurement of the metal's physical and magnetic properties.

J. E. Geusic, H. M. Marcos, and L. G. Van Uitert develop the Nd:YAG laser (neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet) at Bell Labs — one of the most versatile and widely used solid-state lasers in science and industry. Nd:YAG is used for eye surgery, metal welding, rangefinders, and scientific research.

Masato Sagawa at Sumitomo Special Metals in Japan and John Croat at General Motors independently discover neodymium-iron-boron (Nd₂Fe₁₄B) permanent magnets — the strongest permanent magnets known. These magnets revolutionize electric motors, generators, speakers, hard drives, and eventually become essential to every electric vehicle and wind turbine motor.

Demand for neodymium magnets surges as electric vehicles, wind turbines, and consumer electronics expand rapidly. China — which produces approximately 60% of the world's rare earth elements — restricts exports, causing a rare earth crisis that drives international efforts to develop alternative supply chains and recycling programs.

story: Of all the lanthanides, neodymium may have done the most to change the modern world. The Nd₂Fe₁₄B permanent magnet discovered in 1982 is stronger than any previous magnet material — light enough to be practical in electric motors yet powerful enough to replace much larger electromagnets. The electric vehicle revolution is built on neodymium magnets: every EV traction motor contains them, typically 1–2 kg per vehicle. Wind turbine generators also rely on them. The 'new twin' that Welsbach extracted from a false element in 1885 has become one of the most geopolitically significant materials of the 21st century — nations compete for access to neodymium with the same urgency once reserved for oil.
source_note: Discovered by Welsbach (1885) by separation of didymium. Named from Greek neos (new) + didymos (twin). Published in Monatshefte für Chemie, 1885. Nd₂Fe₁₄B magnet discovered by Sagawa and Croat (1982).
